full_response:
We begin with the line element written in the primed, Minkowski–Cartesian coordinates  

(1) ds² = η_{μ'ν'} dx^{μ'} dx^{ν'},  η_{μ'ν'} = diag(−1, +1, +1, +1), μ',ν' = 0…3.  

(The repeated indices μ′, ν′ are to be summed over according to the Einstein summation convention.)

Step 1 :  Express the differentials dx^{μ'} in terms of the un-primed coordinates x^α.  
By the chain rule

(2) dx^{μ'} = (∂x^{μ'} / ∂x^{α}) dx^{α},  α = 0…3.

Step 2 :  Substitute (2) into (1).  
Insert two copies of the chain rule expression because two differentials appear in ds²:

ds² = η_{μ'ν'} (∂x^{μ'} / ∂x^{α}) (∂x^{ν'} / ∂x^{β}) dx^{α} dx^{β}. (3)

Step 3 :  Identify the metric components in the un-primed system.  
Comparing (3) with the general form ds² = g_{αβ} dx^{α} dx^{β}, we read off

(4) g_{αβ}(x) = η_{μ'ν'} (∂x^{μ'} / ∂x^{α}) (∂x^{ν'} / ∂x^{β}).

Equation (4) is completely general: starting from a set of Cartesian (Minkowski) coordinates {x^{μ′}} and performing an arbitrary smooth coordinate transformation to {x^α}, the transformed metric components g_{αβ} are obtained by contracting the flat-space metric η_{μ'ν'} with two Jacobian matrices.

Putting everything together we may therefore write

ds² = η_{μ'ν'} (∂x^{μ'} / ∂x^{α}) (∂x^{ν'} / ∂x^{β}) dx^{α} dx^{β}.  

This formula automatically sums over μ′ and ν′ as well as over α and β when the indices are repeated.

\boxed{\,ds^{2}=η_{\mu' \nu'}\;\frac{\partial x^{\mu'}}{\partial x^{\alpha}}\;\frac{\partial x^{\nu'}}{\partial x^{\beta}}\;dx^{\alpha}\,dx^{\beta}\,}

Confidence: 86%
